data.title: Helen of Troy outlines $1.74B–$1.78B sales target for FY26 amid leadership transition and tariff mitigation
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Helen of Troy Limited (HELE) Q2 2026

Management View

  • Helen of Troy introduced Scott Uzzell as its new Chief Executive Officer, who emphasized his background in consumer-centric leadership and his goal to engineer a comeback for the company by focusing on innovation, brand revitalization, and cultural renewal. Uzzell stated, "I am clear-eyed about the challenges we are facing. I embrace the opportunity to reverse course... Our focus is to invigorate the exceptional assets we have to leverage our leadership brands and talent within our organization to win in the marketplace." He outlined four initial priorities: reenergizing brands and people, placing the consumer at the center, strengthening the portfolio for predictable growth, and improving asset efficiency while maintaining shareholder-friendly policies.
  • CFO Brian Grass welcomed Uzzell and highlighted early progress on five key priorities set last quarter, including improved go-to-market effectiveness and operational excellence. Grass noted, "While we are not at all satisfied with our results, we believe we took a step in the right direction with net sales and adjusted EPS at or above the high end of our outlook ranges." He referenced double-digit revenue growth for Hot Tools, Curlsmith, and Osprey, along with 15% DTC revenue growth year-over-year and positive free cash flow of $23 million fiscal year-to-date, despite a $34 million cash flow drag from tariffs.
  • Assistant CFO Tracy Schuerman confirmed continued focus on tariff mitigation and cost discipline, stating, "We now anticipate that we can lower our cost of goods sold subject to China tariffs to between 25% and 30% by the end of fiscal '26 as compared to our previous expectation of below 25%."

Outlook

  • The company provided a FY26 net sales outlook of $1.74 billion to $1.78 billion, which represents a decline of 8.8% to 6.7% year-over-year. Segment-wise, Home & Outdoor is expected to decline 11.8% to 9.7%, and Beauty & Wellness by 6.2% to 4%, including $130 million to $137 million from Olive & June. Full-year adjusted EPS is projected in the range of $3.75 to $4.25, a decline of 47.7% to 40.7% year-over-year.
  • For Q3, the company expects net sales between $491 million and $512 million and adjusted diluted EPS between $1.55 and $1.80. Management cited ongoing trade disruption, cautious retailer inventory management, and continued soft consumer demand, but expects mitigation from pricing actions and the Olive & June acquisition.

Financial Results

  • Consolidated net sales for the second quarter decreased 8.9%. Excluding Olive & June, organic net sales declined 16%, with about 30% of this attributed to tariff-related disruptions. Olive & June contributed $33.4 million in sales for the quarter.
  • Gross profit margin fell 140 basis points to 44.2%, with higher tariffs on COGS unfavorably impacting margin by approximately 200 basis points. Adjusted operating margin decreased 360 basis points to 6.2%. Non-GAAP adjusted EPS was $0.59, compared to $1.21 in the prior year period.
  • Inventory ended at $528.9 million, up $59 million year-over-year, impacted by the Olive & June acquisition and $32 million of tariff-related costs. Total debt stood at $893.2 million, a sequential increase of $22 million.

Q&A

  • Rupesh Parikh, Oppenheimer & Co.: Asked about the portfolio and divestiture opportunities. Uzzell responded that all brands have promise and that specific decisions will be evaluated as part of the long-range plan, but no immediate changes are planned.
  • Parikh followed up on the earnings base outlook. Grass said there are transitory impacts in FY26 that are expected to dissipate in the second half and into FY27, serving as building blocks for growth.
  • Bob Labick, CJS Securities: Inquired about revitalizing leading brands. Uzzell emphasized "obsessing the consumer and consumer insights," driving innovation, and improving decision-making speed.
  • Labick asked about leverage and capital structure. Grass said the optimal leverage target is closer to 2x, and discussions with banks have been supportive regarding potential covenant relief.
  • Susan Anderson, Canaccord Genuity: Asked about growth opportunities by segment. Uzzell and Grass highlighted upside in both Home & Outdoor and Beauty & Wellness, as well as M&A potential once the foundation is strengthened.
  • Anderson asked for color on performance within segments. Grass noted wellness as weaker due to direct import disruption, while OXO and Osprey had favorable point-of-sale, and insulated beverageware remained soft.
  • Olivia Tong Cheang, Raymond James: Asked about innovation priorities and discounting in drinkware. Uzzell sees opportunities across the portfolio, prioritizing consumer-centric innovation. Grass discussed normalization in the hydration category and focus on bottles, noting distribution opportunities.
  • Tong Cheang also probed tariff mitigation confidence. Grass said, "We've pulled a lot of the levers, feel good about where we've ended up from a price increase perspective. Only thing that we're being cautious about is how the consumer responds and the elasticity of it."
  • An analyst for Peter Grom, UBS: Asked about volume stabilization assumptions. Schuerman said the outlook assumes "kind of the consistent soft demand trend that we're seeing in the first half" and expects inventory rebalancing and direct import recovery in the second half.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a neutral to slightly cautious tone, pressing on portfolio decisions, earnings base, and margin recovery, with questions focused on transitory impacts and volume stabilization.
  • Management's prepared remarks were cautiously optimistic and focused on renewal and execution, while Q&A responses were transparent about ongoing challenges but stressed confidence in mitigation plans. Uzzell's closing remarks maintained a forward-looking, "bullish and excited" tone.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management showed modestly improved confidence, with less defensiveness and a greater focus on strategic initiatives and long-term recovery.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter featured a CEO transition, bringing renewed focus on culture, innovation, and simplification, in addition to continued emphasis on tariff mitigation and operational effectiveness.
  • Guidance language shifted from providing only quarterly outlooks to offering a full-year forecast, despite ongoing trade disruption.
  • Key metrics such as gross margin, operating margin, and adjusted EPS showed further declines, but management highlighted improved DTC growth and progress on cost controls.
  • Analysts' tone evolved from concern over the depth and duration of tariff impacts to more future-oriented questions about brand revitalization and earnings recovery.
  • Management's tone was more confident, reflecting early progress on strategic priorities and leadership transition, in contrast to the more cautious and defensive stance in the previous quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited ongoing trade disruption, tariff-related revenue and cost impacts, cautious consumer behavior, and soft demand as major headwinds.
  • Tariff mitigation actions include supplier diversification, inventory management, supplier cost reductions, price increases, and cost management.
  • Management acknowledged that inventory and leverage remain higher than desired and that they may proactively seek additional flexibility from lenders.
  • Analysts repeatedly raised concerns about volume stabilization, elasticity of demand, and the competitive promotional environment, particularly in the hydration category.

Final Takeaway

Helen of Troy's Q2 2026 call marked a leadership handoff and a renewed commitment to innovation, operating effectiveness, and portfolio revitalization. Management acknowledged persistent headwinds from tariffs and soft demand, but pointed to improving operational alignment, robust DTC growth, and targeted pricing actions as key steps toward stabilization. The company set a full-year sales target of $1.74 billion to $1.78 billion and outlined a path toward long-term growth, supported by cost discipline, supply chain diversification, and a sharpened focus on consumer-centric brand building.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Alliance Laundry pops in its IPO open to exceed Whirlpool's market cap
data.description:
Alliance Laundry Holdings (NYSE:ALH) opened for trading on Thursday at $24.50 after the company priced its upsized IPO at $22 per share. The offering included 24.4 million shares sold by the company and 13.2 million shares sold by an existing selling stockholder. Alliance Laundry (NYSE:ALH) will not receive proceeds from the selling stockholder's portion of the sale.  

Shares of Alliance Laundry (ALH) traded as high as $24.60 in early action to value the company above $4.5 billion, just above the market cap of Whirlpool (WHR).

Alliance Laundry Holdings (ALH) is known as the world's leading producer of commercial washing machines and dryers. The Ripon, Wisconsin-based company already has new investors Capital International Investors and Kayne Anderson Rudnick committed to purchasing $200 million worth of shares in the offering.

The company designs and manufactures commercial laundry systems used in healthcare facilities, hotels, laundromats, fire stations, and other institutional settings. In its SEC filing, Alliance Laundry (ALH) said it held an estimated 40% share of the North American commercial laundry market and maintains positions in other international markets.

Alliance Laundry Holdings (ALH) booked $1.6 billion in revenue for the 12 months ended June 30. Net income of $79.0 million was booked for the same period. The company highlighted in its SEC filing that it has achieved a compound annual revenue growth rate of about 9.5% since 2010 and maintains adjusted EBITDA margins around 25%.

Major industry players that compete with Alliance Laundry (ALH) to various degrees include Whirlpool (WHR), Samsung (OTCPK:SSNLF), LG Electronics, Electrolux AB (OTCPK:ELUXY), Maytag (WHR), Kenmore, and General Electric (GE).

data.title: Cross Country Healthcare falls amid report on FTC review of Aya deal
data.description:
Cross Country Healthcare (NASDAQ:CCRN) dropped 5% amid a report about the status of the Federal Trade Commission's review of its planned sale to Aya Healthcare.

The FTC has been deposing third parties in its review of the planned $615 million transaction, according to traders, who cited a Capitol Forum report that circulated on Thursday. The antitrust regulator is focusing on the impact of rival travel nursing staffing companies combining. The FTC staff is largely focused on how the combined company would impact hospitals. 

In March, Cross Country (NASDAQ:CCRN) received a second request from the FTC about the travel nursing company's planned sale to Aya. Cross Country (NASDAQ:CCRN) and Aya disclosed the companies had certified that they had substantially complied with the FTC's second request as of Aug. 29, according to an 8-K filing. The deal is expected to close in Q4.

Aya Healthcare in December entered into a definitive agreement to acquire Cross Country for $18.61/share in cash.

data.title: AstraZeneca eyes $500M additional investment in new Virginia plant
data.description:
AstraZeneca (NASDAQ:AZN) on Thursday announced a $4.5B investment in a proposed manufacturing site in Virginia, $500M more than it initially planned, as the U.K.-based company becomes the latest pharma giant to expand its U.S. footprint amid the Trump administration’s tariff threats.

The facility, expected to be operational within the next four to five years, is part of the Anglo-Swedish drugmaker’s plans to invest $50B in the U.S. by 2030 to improve its manufacturing and R&D operations.

The site, located in Albemarle County, will focus on producing drug substances for its obesity and metabolic portfolio, the company said, adding that it will create 600 highly skilled jobs, including 100 more jobs due to the additional investment.

“Today's groundbreaking demonstrates the Trump Administration's commitment to onshoring drug manufacturing and strengthening supply chains to improve Americans' access to medication,” said Mehmet Oz, Administrator of the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS).

The investment, which Astra (NASDAQ:AZN) CEO Pascal Soriot identified as the company’s largest in history, comes after Pfizer (PFE) received a three-year reprieve on tariffs from the Trump administration last week in exchange for additional investments in the U.S.

data.title: Tilray reaffirms $62M-$72M adjusted EBITDA guidance as international cannabis and beverage integration progress
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Tilray Brands, Inc. (TLRY) Q1 2026

Management View

  • CEO Irwin Simon highlighted that "Q1 of fiscal 2026 was a testament to the significant momentum Tilray has built across our businesses over the years," pointing to sustainable growth and operational efficiency as core results drivers. He noted "our stock [regaining] strength this quarter and return to full NASDAQ compliance," and emphasized shareholder support as critical.
  • Simon reported "net income of $1.5 million and earnings per share of 0," underscoring a commitment to profitability. He stated, "total revenue increased by 5% year-over-year to a Q1 record net revenue of $210 million," driven by double-digit growth in Canadian Adult-Use and International Cannabis businesses.
  • The company continues to optimize its craft beer SKU portfolio under Project 420, with beverage segment revenue remaining flat due to deliberate portfolio decisions.
  • Tilray's balance sheet strengthened further with a reduction in outstanding debt by $7.7 million and cash and equivalents at $265 million.
  • Simon described multiple growth opportunities, including leveraging CC Pharma's distribution in Germany, expanding internationally in cannabis and beverages, and entering the U.S. hemp-derived THC beverage market.
  • CFO Carl Merton stated, "We are reporting record first quarter net revenue, net income and a significantly improved adjusted free cash flow for the period. Further, we are reaffirming our 2026 guidance for adjusted EBITDA."

Outlook

  • The company reaffirmed its 2026 guidance, anticipating adjusted EBITDA between $62 million and $72 million.
  • Management expressed confidence in international growth, particularly in Germany, where medical cannabis distribution is expected to triple in fiscal 2026.
  • Simon indicated optimism about regulatory changes in the U.S. and Europe that could drive future growth: "I fully expect positive change ahead, and I'm certain in our ability to adapt swiftly and we will strategically."

Financial Results

  • Net revenue for the first quarter was $210 million, a 5% increase year-over-year.
  • Cannabis revenue reached $64.5 million, up 5% year-over-year, with adult-use gross revenue up 12% and international cannabis up 10%.
  • Beverage revenue was $55.7 million, impacted by ongoing SKU rationalization and craft brand integration.
  • Wellness revenue grew 3% year-over-year to $15.2 million, supported by new product launches and expanded retail presence.
  • Distribution revenue increased 9% year-over-year to $74 million, attributed primarily to a stronger euro.
  • Gross profit for the quarter was $57.5 million compared to $59.7 million last year, with gross margin at 27% versus 30% previously. Segment margins declined in cannabis and beverages, while wellness margins were flat and distribution margins dipped slightly.
  • Net income was $1.5 million or $0.00 per share, compared to a net loss of $34.7 million in the prior year period.
  • Adjusted EBITDA for the quarter was $10.2 million, up from $9.3 million last year.
  • Cash flow used in operations improved significantly to negative $1.3 million from negative $35.3 million last year.

Q&A

  • Aaron Grey, Alliance Global Partners, asked about the impact of permit delays in Portugal and the strategy for expanding CC Pharma's distribution in Germany. Irwin Simon explained the company is "finally seeing permits coming through" in Portugal and expects German quota increases to shift business from the second to third quarter, while integrating CC Pharma to distribute more medical cannabis in Germany.
  • Grey also inquired about Tilray's readiness for a U.S. rescheduling scenario. Simon indicated the company could leverage its "current infrastructure, current people, current know-how, current growth, current research, current genetics for medicine or partnering with a pharma company or buying something is something we're open and ready to do."
  • William Kirk, ROTH Capital, asked about digital assets strategy. CIO Lloyd Brathwaite stated, "We actually invested in Bitcoin, and we're also looking at some other assets such as Ethereum and Solana... enabling our websites so that we can actually accept Bitcoin."
  • Xin Ma, TD Cowen, asked about Canadian adult-use market maturity and pricing power. President of Tilray Canada, Blair MacNeil, responded, "In the quarter, we saw overall market pricing down 1.3% and volume was up 6.5%. For us, our pricing was actually up 2%, and our volume was ahead of the market."
  • Ma also questioned improvement in beverage gross margin. Simon outlined ongoing efforts, "We've taken $25 million of cost out, I mean, there's more to go... there's a lot of wood to chop there yet to get those margins to where we need to do."
  • Frederico Gomes, ATB Capital, asked about supply chain risk in Portugal and German regulatory changes. Simon noted the company remains committed to Portugal while maintaining flexibility with facilities in Germany and Canada, and is closely monitoring legislative developments in Germany.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' tone was focused and probing, especially around international regulatory risk, margin recovery, and operational execution, reflecting a neutral to slightly positive sentiment with an eye on operational hurdles.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A, frequently emphasizing progress, strategic positioning, and adaptability. Simon's language remained optimistic: "I'm highly confident in Tilray's outlook for the remainder of 2026 and beyond."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analysts remained inquisitive but less skeptical, while management's tone shifted from defensive (addressing impairments and challenges) to more upbeat, underlining progress on debt, cash flow, and operational integration.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q1 2026 featured a return to net income and positive operational cash flow, in contrast to the prior quarter’s emphasis on noncash impairments and restructuring.
  • Revenue growth for the quarter was more broad-based, with all segments except beverages growing. The previous quarter saw a heavier focus on beverage underperformance and SKU rationalization.
  • Guidance language was consistent, with reaffirmation of the $62 million to $72 million adjusted EBITDA target.
  • Management’s confidence improved, shifting from explaining setbacks and impairments to discussing growth opportunities and operational momentum.
  • Analysts' focus moved from regulatory delays and cost cuts to international expansion and market share gains.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited regulatory delays in Portugal and quota limits in Germany as ongoing challenges but noted recent progress in permit acquisition.
  • Margin pressures in both cannabis and beverage segments were highlighted, with management attributing declines to product mix and integration costs but expressing confidence in long-term margin recovery.
  • Analysts remained focused on international supply chain risks, competitive pressures in Canadian cannabis, and the timeline for operational improvements in beverages.

Final Takeaway

Tilray’s Q1 2026 results reflected robust revenue growth, a return to net income, and significant improvement in operational cash flow. Management reaffirmed full-year adjusted EBITDA guidance and underscored confidence in international expansion, especially in Germany and the U.S., while continuing to optimize its diversified cannabis and beverage businesses. Margin pressure and regulatory risks remain, but ongoing integration and portfolio rationalization support the company’s roadmap for sustainable growth.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Cooler, wetter autumn weather will drive traffic to Tractor Supply -- Citi
data.description:
• Tractor Supply’s (NASDAQ:TSCO) recent pullback represents an “attractive entry point,” says Citi Research analyst Steven Zaccone, as steady traffic, pricing tailwinds, and new initiatives will lead to upside in third quarter earnings.
  • “Tractor Supply is a defensive growth retailer not dependent upon a housing market recovery to return to growth…and is trading at a discount to the retail basket,” Zaccone adds, driving and an upgrade in the stock to Buy from Hold and 3% bump to its price target to $62.  
  • “Weather has a strong impact on Tractor Supply," considering the company’s farm and ranch clientele.
  • After an unseasonably warm and dry start to autumn, a cooler and wetter October should contribute to improved same store sales, Zaccone says in support of his bullish rating.
  • The company will report third quarter results before the open on October 23, expected to show a profit of $0.48 per share on $3.72B in sales, an increase of 6.6% and 7.2% from a year ago, respectively.

data.title: U.S. rare earth miners surge as China tightens export controls
data.description:
Shares of American rare-earth mining companies extended their rally Thursday after Beijing unveiled new restrictions on exports, fueling expectations that U.S. producers will see increased demand and support from Washington.

MP Materials (MP) jumped nearly 11%, USA Rare Earth (USAR) rose more than 20%, and Ramaco Resources (METC) (METCB) gained 14% before giving back some of the gains. The sector has been climbing since early July, when the U.S. government disclosed plans to buy a stake in MP Materials. Both USA Rare Earth and Ramaco have since tripled in value.

China’s Ministry of Commerce announced that exporters will now need licenses for products containing even small amounts of certain rare earths. Analysts warn that such measures could disrupt global supply chains given China’s dominance, accounting for about 90% of downstream rare-earth processing and products, Bloomberg News reported.

TD Cowen’s David Deckelbaum said the move reinforces the investment case for U.S. rare-earth companies, calling the domestic supply chain “a compelling place for capital right now.” He also noted that tightening fundamentals and growing federal involvement should continue to propel the sector.

The White House added momentum earlier this week by revealing that it would purchase a 10% stake in Canada’s Trilogy Metals (TMQ) (TMQ:CA), expanding its holdings in companies linked to critical minerals deemed vital to U.S. security interests. Rare earths are a key component in advanced weapons systems.

data.title: Fed Governor Barr urges caution in adjusting rates due to continued uncertainty
data.description:
Federal Reserve Governor Michael Barr said on Thursday that he's skeptical that the Federal Open Market Committee should "fully 'look through' higher inflation from import tariffs."

The series of recent tariff developments have not been predictable. "At some point, businesses and consumers could start to make pricing, spending, and wage decisions based on their belief in higher future inflation, thereby driving a cycle of persistence," he said in prepared text for a speech at the Economic Club of Minnesota.

As a result, "common sense would indicate that when there is a lot of uncertainty, one should move cautiously." 

In mid-September, the FOMC reduced its policy rate by 25 basis points to 4.00%-4.25%, a move that he supported. "Monetary policy was and remains modestly restrictive, so it seemed to me appropriate to move the rate a bit closer toward neutral, pending more data and further developments on the economy, the forecast, and the balance of risks," he said.

Barr cited the "Brainard Principle" for his careful approach to future moves. The principle "holds that when there is considerable uncertainty about the consequences of a policy action, the recommended course is to move more gradually than would otherwise be the case."

"I believe that principle applies now, and that the FOMC should be cautious about adjusting policy so that we can gather further data, update our forecasts, and better assess the balance of risks," Barr said.

If inflation drifts up further, it may be necessary to keep policy "at least modestly restrictive for longer," he said.  However, "if we see heightened risks in the labor market, then we may need to move more quickly to ease policy."

Developing... check back for updates.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Microsoft Azure deploys first large-scale cluster of Nvidia GB300 for OpenAI workloads
data.description:
Microsoft (NASDAQ:MSFT) Azure has deployed its first large-scale cluster of Nvidia's (NASDAQ:NVDA) GB300 NVL72 systems, which feature more than 4,600 Blackwell Ultra GPUs connected with the Quantum-X800 Infiniband networking platform.

"First of many as we scale to hundreds of thousands of GB300s across our DCs, and rethink every layer of the stack across silicon, systems, and software to support next gen AI workloads," said Microsoft CEO Satya Nadella in a post on X on Thursday. 

"This co-engineered system delivers the world's first at-scale GB300 production cluster, providing the supercomputing engine needed for OpenAI to serve multitrillion-parameter models," said Ian Buck, Nvidia's vice president of Hyperscale and High-performance Computing. "This sets the definitive new standard for accelerated computing."

"Our collaboration helps ensure customers like OpenAI can deploy next-generation infrastructure at unprecedented scale and speed," added Nidhi Chappell, corporate vice president of Microsoft Azure AI Infrastructure.

The system provides 37 terabytes of fast memory and 1.44 exaflops of FP4 Tensor Core performance per VM, creating a large, unified memory space needed for reasoning models, agentic AI systems and multi-modal generative AI.

The announcement today tightens the bond between Nvidia, Microsoft and OpenAI. Last month, Nvidia revealed its plans to invest up to $100B in OpenAI to build and deploy at least 10 gigawatts of AI data centers with Nvidia systems.

Nvidia shares edged up 2% by afternoon trading on Thursday, reaching a new intraday high.

Microsoft continues to invest heavily in its data center footprint. The tech giant reported capital expenditures of $88.7B in fiscal year 2025, which ended June 30. During the first quarter of fiscal 2026, it plans to spend $30B, which is about a 50% year-over-year increase.

data.title: GoodRx spikes after report on plans to join TrumpRx
data.description:
GoodRx (NASDAQ:GDRX) shares added ~17% on Thursday after Reuters reported that the drug savings platform, along with a trade group representing pharmacy operators such as Costco (COST), is in talks with the White House about joining the government’s TrumpRx website.

Last week, President Donald Trump unveiled the direct-to-consumer site expected to launch next year, with Pfizer (PFE) agreeing to initially offer four of its brand-name drugs to cash-paying customers at sharp discounts.  

Announcing the deal at the Oval Office on Tuesday, Trump said other pharma companies are also expected to join the initiative, which comes under his most-favored-nation (MFN) pricing policy.

GoodRx (NASDAQ:GDRX) CEO Wendy Barnes noted that "if we include the pharma direct programs, we also have to find a way to convey competitive cash pricing at retail pharmacies."

According to Reuters, the National Community Pharmacists Association and the National Association of Chain Drug Stores, which represent pharmacy operators such as Costco (COST), have said they were also in discussions with the Trump administration. CVS Health (CVS), one of the largest pharmacy operators in the U.S., exited the National Association of Chain Drug Stores in 2022.

"As long as drugmakers deliver cost savings for American patients through TrumpRx, how they do so is irrelevant," White House spokesman Kush Desai noted.
